topic: Cold Start Patterns — Success and Failure Cases

citations.title: GitHub at RailsConf — GitHub Blog (May 2008)
key_facts: GitHub had a booth at RailsConf 2008 in Portland (Engine Yard's booth)
All four founders spoke in separate sessions
Conference confirmed Rails → GitHub migration was simultaneous with public launch
GitHub positioned as technical/Rails developer tool from day one

citations.title: One Million Repositories — GitHub Blog (July 25, 2010)
key_facts: 1 million repositories reached July 24-25, 2010
~27 months after public launch (April 2008)
Notable projects at milestone: jQuery, Ruby on Rails, Node.js, Redis
First million took 3 years 8 months 15 days total from first commit (Oct 2007)

citations.title: How GitHub Democratized Coding — NIRA
key_facts: First commit: October 19, 2007
PJ Hyett joined January 2008; public launch April 10, 2008
Geoffrey Grosenbach (PeepCode) was first paying user in April 2008 — volunteered payment
Freemium model was reactive, not pre-planned
46,000 repos by February 2009 (first year); 100,000 users by mid-2009
No VC until 2012 ($100M Series A, a16z); profitable on subscriptions alone

quotes.speaker: Chris Wanstrath
quotes.text: In other words, we'd charge the people asking to be charged.

citations.title: Antler Early Days Episode 1: Strava with Mark Gainey
key_facts: Founders Gainey and Horvath met at Harvard rowing in 1980s
Virtual locker room concept dates to 1995 business plan
July 2008 prototype: 20 cyclist friends, Garmin GPS, Tour de France timing
First customer 'David' still with Strava 12-13 years later
2009: 'just the six of them telling their friends and getting them to sign up'
Segments feature emerged organically from user conversations about iconic climbs
Target: MAMILs (middle-aged men in lycra) — passionate, performance-obsessed
Premium price: $6/month
Strategy: 'inch wide, mile deep'

quotes.speaker: Mark Gainey
quotes.text: We had one customer, his name is David, he's still with us 12-13 years later. We had to figure out every time he uploaded to Strava, how did he have fun?

citations.title: Contrary Research: Substack Business Breakdown & Founding Story
key_facts: Founded May 2017: Chris Best (CEO), Jairaj Sethi (CTO), Hamish McKenzie (Chief Writing Officer)
All three met at Kik messaging app
First customer: Bill Bishop (Sinocism) — 30K subscribers, China newsletter
October 18, 2017: Bishop's paywall email brought six figures of revenue by end of day
YC W2018
Paid subscribers: 11K (Jul 2018) → 50K (2019) → 100K (Aug 2020) → 500K (Feb 2021) → 1M (Nov 2021)
Business model: 10% commission + 2.9% + $0.30 Stripe fees
Writers own their subscriber lists (portability)

citations.title: The History of LinkedIn: From 2003 Startup to 1 Billion Users — powerin.io
key_facts: Conceived December 2002 by Reid Hoffman (former PayPal COO) + 4 co-founders
Launch: May 5, 2003
Day 1: ~4,500 members (personal contacts of founders)
December 2003: 81,000 members (organic, invitation mechanics)
April 2004: 500,000 members
August 2004: 1,000,000 members (15 months post-launch)
2010: 100M members
2023: 1B+ members

citations.title: LinkedIn Sequoia Series A Press Release (November 2003)
key_facts: Sequoia Capital led Series A: $4.7M
Pre-money valuation: $10-15M
Investment came November 2003, ~6 months post-launch
~80,000 users at time of Series A

citations.title: LinkedIn Recruiter Launch Press Release (March 2008)
key_facts: LinkedIn Recruiter launched March 2008
Described as 'Corporate Solutions product'
Premium features for corporate staffing departments
Became dominant B2B revenue stream for next decade
LinkedIn Jobs had launched March 2005 as first premium product

citations.title: Axios: Substack says it has more than 1 million paid subscriptions (Nov 15, 2021)
key_facts: 1 million paid subscriptions announced November 2021
Up from 250,000 in December 2020 (4x in 11 months)
Zero profits at this milestone
Top 10 writers: $20M collective annual revenue (up from $7M prior year)
High-profile recruits: Glenn Greenwald, Matt Taibbi, Matt Yglesias, Andrew Sullivan, Marc Stein

citations.title: What Happened To Polywork? An Eye-opening Story Of Fail Growth — Prompt-Led Product Substack
key_facts: Founded 2021 by Peter Johnston; closed January 31, 2025
Raised $13M total (prior to Series B — see V414 for corrected total)
Backers: Stripe Collison brothers, Alexis Ohanian, Elad Gil
Won Product Hunt Golden Kitty Award 2022
Late 2023 pivot to website builder alienated early adopters
Never disclosed user numbers or MRR

citations.title: Peter Johnston X post — Polywork shutdown announcement (@multiplay3r)
key_facts: Shut down after 3.5 years
Returned remaining capital to investors
First 18 months described as 'highlight of career'
Immediately pivoted to new AI startup

quotes.speaker: Peter Johnston
quotes.text: after 3.5 years, I made the call to shut @Polywork down and return the remaining capital to investors

quotes.speaker: Peter Johnston
quotes.text: building consumer social is like doing a startup on Level 900 Difficulty Mode

quotes.speaker: Peter Johnston
quotes.text: no longer saw a viable path to Polywork becoming a gigantic business — and that was what we signed up for

citations.title: Polywork Secures $28M Series B — BusinessWire (September 2022)
key_facts: $28M Series B led by Caffeinated Capital, September 2022
Simultaneous with public beta launch (after 18 months private beta)
Total raised: approximately $41.5-44.5M
Invite-only from 2021 through September 2022

citations.title: TechCrunch: Perplexity acquires Read.cv (January 17, 2025)
key_facts: Founded 2021 by Andy Chung (former Facebook, Mozilla, Quip designer)
~3 employees at acquisition
Backed by F7 Ventures and Fanjul Capital (small amounts, undisclosed)
Perplexity rationale: consumer/social design talent
Platform wound down January 17, 2025; data export deadline May 16, 2025
Third Perplexity acquisition (after Carbon and Spellwise)
100K+ community members self-reported (year unspecified)

quotes.speaker: Aravind Srinivas (Perplexity CEO)
quotes.text: incredibly capable in designing and building consumer and social experiences

citations.title: Hacker News: Read CV Acquired by Perplexity (item #42742241)
key_facts: Predominantly negative sentiment
Core complaint: users used as stepping stones to founder liquidity
Andy Chung called announcement 'incredibly bittersweet'
Users immediately organized alternatives: Reactive Resume, twigg.social, tini.bio
Some credit given for Next.js data export option

quotes.speaker: JadoJodo (HN user)
quotes.text: Users are increasingly treated like stepping stools for founders...users help grow a product...get comfortable with that product, integrate it into their lives...and then it's yanked out from under them.

quotes.speaker: pixeldrifter (HN user)
quotes.context: Describing the acquisition as focused on cashing out rather than serving community

quotes.speaker: dorian-graph (HN user)
quotes.text: Yet again, let down by a centralised platform
quotes.context: Recurring disappointment with startup social platforms

quotes.speaker: Andy Chung (founder)
quotes.text: incredibly bittersweet for us, as the start of this new chapter will mark the end of our time with @read_cv

citations.title: LinkedIn Pulse: The quiet demise of Lunchclub (Tony Shepherd)
key_facts: Lunchclub declined gradually, not dramatically — no collective mourning
Core limitation: AI matches profiles, struggles to convey reputational signal
Senior professionals require trust through context, not algorithmic serendipity
No mechanism to deepen relationships beyond initial introduction
Post-COVID normalization removed primary use case
Users quietly migrated; no successor platform emerged

quotes.speaker: Article author (Tony Shepherd)
quotes.text: AI can match profiles. It struggles to convey reputational signal.
quotes.context: Core structural limitation of Lunchclub's model

quotes.speaker: Article author
quotes.text: maturity often looks like subtraction, not replacement
quotes.context: Describing how senior professionals abandoned the platform

citations.title: CNBC: Lunchclub tops $100M valuation amid quarantine usage spike (September 2020)
key_facts: Founded November 2017 by Vladimir Novakovski, Scott Wu, Hayley Leibson
Seed: $4M from a16z
Series A: $24M from Coatue and Lightspeed (September 2020)
Valuation: $100M+ post-Series A
Team size at peak: 15 people
Added video chat March 2020 (COVID adaptation)
No specific MAU/DAU ever disclosed publicly

citations.title: Tech.co: My Path Timeline is Telling of Why the App Failed (2015)
key_facts: Launched November 14, 2010; founders: Dave Morin, Dustin Mierau, Shawn Fanning
1.5M downloads in first 3 weeks
Google offered $100M at 3 months — Morin declined
2M users after November 2011 redesign
February 2012: privacy scandal (uploading address books without permission)
February 2013: FTC $800K fine for underage data storage
2014: lost most Western users; pivoted to Korea/Indonesia
May 2015: sold to Daum Kakao (price undisclosed)
September 2018: platform shut down
Total raised: $66.2M burned through

citations.title: TechCrunch: Path Sells to Daum Kakao (May 2015)
key_facts: Path sold to Daum Kakao on May 28, 2015
Acquisition price: undisclosed (the $80M figure in research brief is unconfirmed by any primary source)
Dave Morin departed post-acquisition
Daum Kakao: South Korean internet conglomerate (now Kakao Corp)

citations.title: Contra: $30M Series B announcement (Hunt Scanlon / PR Newswire 2022)
key_facts: Founded 2019 by Ben Huffman and Gajus Kuizinas
Series A: $14.5M from NEA and Unusual Ventures
Series B: $30M from NEA (January 2022); total ~$44.5M
1M+ registered users claimed at Series B
November 2021: launched 100% commission-free payments as differentiator
Platform still active as of 2026 but community sentiment declining

citations.title: Alejandro Cremades interview with Ben Huffman (Contra founder)
key_facts: Ben Huffman's background: early Stripe employee (fintech expertise)
Mission: give independent workers freedom without commission fees
Commission-free as primary differentiator
Raised $44M total to build the freelance platform for independent workers

citations.title: Strava Wikipedia — Growth milestones and mobile expansion
key_facts: Founded 2009, San Francisco; seed investors: Sigma Partners
2011: iOS and Android apps launched — removed Garmin dependency
2012: Running added — tripled addressable market
2016: 20 million users
2017: 28 million users; ~1M new members every 40 days
2018: 36 million; 2019: 42 million

high_confidence: GitHub: sourced from GitHub Blog primary posts + NIRA secondary
LinkedIn: confirmed from official press releases (Sequoia Nov 2003, Greylock Oct 2004, Recruiter Mar 2008)
Substack: confirmed from Contrary Research + Axios + Nasdaq for 1M milestone
Read.cv: TechCrunch primary + HN community thread
medium_confidence: Strava 1M users date: not confirmed precisely; estimated 2012-2013 based on mobile launch + running expansion
Polywork $1.45B valuation: cited in research brief but not confirmed in any public source found
Peter Johnston X post text: URL paywalled; content from secondary sources
low_confidence_or_unconfirmed: Path acquisition price ($80M): no primary or secondary source confirmed this figure; Daum Kakao never disclosed
Lunchclub formal shutdown: platform technically still live as of 2026; 'quiet demise' not official closure
Contra current fee structure: no public announcement of commission reversal found
